title: Average Order Value (AOV)
business_definition: AOV = total revenue / number of orders. Rising AOV means customers are buying more per transaction. Falling AOV can signal pricing pressure, mix shift to cheaper products, or promotional discounting.
diagnostic_questions: Did AOV drop because of product mix shift (more cheap items) or lower prices overall?
Are discounts or promotions being applied more frequently?
Did a specific category or segment drive the AOV change?
Are customers buying fewer items per order (units per transaction dropped)?

causal_relationships.detection_sql: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS month, AVG(amount) AS aov, AVG(discount_amount / NULLIF(amount, 0)) AS avg_discount_rate, AVG(item_count) AS avg_items FROM orders WHERE status = 'completed' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1

causal_relationships.symptom: AOV varies significantly by customer segment
causal_relationships.detection_sql: SELECT customer_segment, COUNT(*) AS orders, AVG(amount) AS aov, STDDEV(amount) AS aov_stddev FROM orders JOIN customers USING (customer_id) WHERE order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC

inflation_causes.cause: Bulk or wholesale orders included — inflating retail AOV
inflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT order_type, COUNT(*), AVG(amount) FROM orders GROUP BY 1

deflation_causes.cause: Shipping and taxes stripped from order amount inconsistently
deflation_causes.detection_sql: SELECT AVG(gross_amount) AS avg_gross, AVG(net_amount) AS avg_net, AVG(shipping) AS avg_shipping FROM orders WHERE order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days'

anti_patterns: Do not include cancelled or returned orders in AOV calculation
Do not compare AOV across B2B and B2C segments without separating them first

sql_assets.aov_trend: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('week', order_date) AS week, AVG(amount) AS aov, COUNT(*) AS orders FROM orders WHERE status = 'completed' AND order_date >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '90 days' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1
